input.content:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
root@6d34054701b6:/app#

{
  "analysis": "We are at /app with no prior command output. The task is to diagnose and fix resume-audit mismatches (illegal moves/visibility) so that running the matrix script produces /app/output/resume-audit.json where all cases are ok, illegal counts are 0, signatures reconcile as specified, and the JSON matches the records JSONL and log rows. First step is to run the matrix to reproduce the issue, inspect the audit output, then narrow to failing cases using one-case runs, and finally inspect relevant maps/scripts/config and engine code to fix resume path divergence.",
  "plan": "1) Run the full matrix to generate resume-audit.json. 2) Summarize which cases fail and why by inspecting ok/illegal/sig and any log hints. 3) For failing cases, run one-case to reproduce in isolation and compare fresh vs resume move lists. 4) Validate that records JSONL and log match the JSON for a failing case to understand whether the problem is in generation or serialization. After we identify the first concrete failing discrepancy, we can inspect the engine code path for resume vs fresh (likely state/visibility/blocked-tile handling) and patch; but first we need the failing details.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh
",
      "duration": 10.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la /app/output
",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
p='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(p) as f: data=json.load(f)
cases=data.get('cases',[])
print('cases:', [c.get('name') for c in cases])
for c in cases:
    name=c.get('name')
    ok=c.get('ok')
    fresh=c.get('fresh',{})
    resume=c.get('resume',{})
    fi=fresh.get('illegal'); ri=resume.get('illegal')
    fs=fresh.get('sig'); rs=resume.get('sig')
    fsteps=fresh.get('steps'); rsteps=resume.get('steps')
    if not ok or fi or ri or (fs!=rs and fs and rs):
        print('\nFAIL:', name)
        print(' ok:', ok)
        print(' fresh illegal/steps/sig:', fi, fsteps, fs)
        print(' resume illegal/steps/sig:', ri, rsteps, rs)
        print(' records:', c.get('records'))
        print(' log:', c.get('log'))
        print(' visibility:', c.get('visibility'))
        print(' effects:', c.get('effects'))
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": false
}

root@6d34054701b6:/app#

{
  "analysis": "Matrix run completes and produces /app/output/resume-audit.json. Two cases fail: rook and sentinel. Both have illegal=0 and steps=1 in both paths, but fresh vs resume sig differ. Visibility differs too (fresh_seen 9 vs resume_seen 8), suggesting resume path is missing a visible tile / line-of-sight update. Sentinel additionally has an active 'ward' effect (epoch 7), which may be related to visibility/state rebuild on resume. Next we need to diff the move lists between fresh and resume for these cases, and verify whether JSON/records/log disagree or if the engine produced different resolved moves. Then isolate with one-case runs and inspect their workspace trace/records.",
  "plan": "1) Inspect rook/sentinel case entries in the audit to see fresh/resume moves and any per-move legal flags. 2) Validate that the records.jsonl and trace.tsv for rook and sentinel match the moves listed in the audit (to ensure serialization is consistent). 3) Run one-case for rook and sentinel to reproduce in isolation and compare to the matrix row. 4) If differences persist, inspect the input definitions for rook/sentinel (maps/scripts/config) and then locate engine code handling resume state/visibility/effects.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
p='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(p) as f: data=json.load(f)
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    c=next(x for x in data['cases'] if x['name']==name)
    print('\n==',name,'==')
    print('ok',c['ok'])
    print('fresh',c['fresh'])
    print('resume',c['resume'])
    print('moves:', json.dumps(c.get('moves',[]), indent=2))
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
from pathlib import Path

def load_jsonl(p):
    rows=[]
    for line in Path(p).read_text().splitlines():
        if not line.strip():
            continue
        rows.append(json.loads(line))
    return rows

a='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(a) as f: data=json.load(f)
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    c=next(x for x in data['cases'] if x['name']==name)
    moves=c.get('moves',[])
    recp=c['records']
    logp=c['log']
    recs=load_jsonl(recp)
    print('\n==',name,'==')
    print('moves',len(moves),'records',len(recs))
    print('moves==records:', moves==recs)
    # show log rows count (nonblank)
    log_lines=[ln for ln in Path(logp).read_text().splitlines() if ln.strip()]
    print('log rows:', len(log_lines))
    if moves!=recs:
        for i,(m,r) in enumerate(zip(moves,recs)):
            if m!=r:
                print('first diff at',i)
                print(' move',m)
                print(' rec ',r)
                break
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh rook
",
      "duration": 5.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh sentinel
",
      "duration": 5.0
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "python3 - <<'PY'
import json
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    p=f'/app/output/single-{name}.json'
    with open(p) as f: data=json.load(f)
    c=data['cases'][0]
    print('\n== single',name,'==')
    print('ok',c['ok'])
    print('fresh',c['fresh'])
    print('resume',c['resume'])
    print('visibility',c.get('visibility'))
    print('effects',c.get('effects'))
    print('moves',json.dumps(c.get('moves',[]), indent=2))
PY
",
      "duration": 1.0
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": false
}

      "duration": 0.1

  "task_complete": true

import json
from pathlib import Path

def load_jsonl(p):
    rows=[]
    for line in Path(p).read_text().splitlines():
        if not line.strip():
            continue
        rows.append(json.loads(line))
    return rows

a='/app/output/resume-audit.json'
with open(a) as f: data=json.load(f)
for name in ['rook','sentinel']:
    c=next(x for x in data['cases'] if x['name']==name)
    moves=c.get('moves',[])
    recp=c['records']
    logp=c['log']
    recs=load_jsonl(recp)
    print('\n==',name,'==')
    print('moves',len(moves),'records',len(recs))
    print('moves==records:', moves==recs)
    # show log rows count (nonblank)
    log_lines=[ln for ln in Path(logp).read_text().splitlines() if ln.strip()]
    print('log rows:', len(log_lines))
    if moves!=recs:
        for i,(m,r) in enumerate(zip(moves,recs)):
            if m!=r:
                print('first diff at',i)
                print(' move',m)
                print(' rec ',r)
                break
PY

You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
The local turn-based grid engine under `/app` produces resume audits where opposing sides step through blocked tiles or act from positions the human cannot currently see, but uninterrupted runs stay clean. Running `bash /app/scripts/run-matrix.sh` writes `/app/output/resume-audit.json`. Bundled layout maps under `/app/data`, action scripts under `/app/data/scripts`, and tables under `/app/config` define the inputs each case name uses; a correct audit reconciles both execution paths against those inputs.

The audit JSON has a top-level cases array for rook, bishop, knight, lancer, sentinel, and warden. Each case uses name, ok, fresh, resume, moves, visibility, effects, records, and log. The fresh and resume summaries describe the same scenario through the two execution paths and carry illegal, steps, and sig. The sig value is the engine signature over the resolved move list for that path, not a new per-run identifier. Each move entry carries turn, side id, from coordinates, to coordinates, kind, and legal.

Records points to a JSONL file whose nonblank rows parse to the same list in the same order. Log points to whitespace-delimited rows in the same order. The audit is clean when every case has ok true, both summaries report illegal 0, signatures match when both paths ran opposing turns, and the JSON, records file, and log file all describe the same resolved rows.

The matrix run also leaves `/app/bin/rogctl` executable. Running `bash /app/scripts/one-case.sh <case>` writes `/app/output/single-<case>.json` with the same top-level cases wrapper and the one requested row, matching that row from the matrix audit.
